tasks.title: WAVE01 Config: Replace setup aliases with owned application-policy config types
tasks.prompt: Use `lib/design/design-plan.json` and `../socialpredict/README/PRODUCTION-NOTES/BACKEND/01-configuration-management.md` as the binding design context for WAVE01 configuration-management. Complete the first useful slice by making `backend/internal/service/config` own application-policy configuration types instead of aliasing `backend/setup` types. Preserve current runtime behavior while introducing an explicit loader seam and tests that prove the owned types can be exercised without `setup` globals, YAML parsing, or `init()` side effects. Keep this task focused on the config service boundary itself; do not yet migrate every downstream consumer.
acceptance_criteria: `backend/internal/service/config` no longer depends on type aliases to `backend/setup` for its public owned types.
Configuration loading is expressed through an explicit loader seam that preserves current source-asset behavior while clarifying ownership.
Focused tests cover the owned config types and loader behavior without relying on `setup` globals or `init()`-driven access.

focus: dependency direction away from setup aliases
tests that avoid legacy singleton behavior

out_of_scope: migrating all handlers and domain services in this task
notes: This is WAVE01 only: configuration-management boundary completion.
Preserve `backend/setup/setup.yaml` as the migration-time source asset.
Do not introduce a new generic top-level `config/` subsystem.

tasks.title: WAVE01 Config: Reduce backend/setup to a source-asset and translation seam
tasks.prompt: After the owned config types exist, reduce `backend/setup` toward the design-plan target role: source asset and translation seam only. Remove or isolate `init()`-driven loading, package-global convenience access, and other singleton behavior from the migrated runtime path while preserving current behavior for the backend entrypoint. The goal is to stop `setup` from acting as the long-term runtime boundary. Keep the migration reversible and avoid a flag-day rewrite.
acceptance_criteria: The migrated runtime path no longer depends on `setup` package-global accessors or `init()`-driven loading.
`backend/setup` is narrowed toward source-asset and translation responsibilities for the touched path.
Focused tests demonstrate that runtime configuration initialization works through explicit seams rather than singleton state.

focus: removal of migrated global access paths

out_of_scope: domain-consumer migration beyond touched runtime paths
notes: Treat `setup` as transitional, not final architecture.
Keep startup validation explicit and fail-fast.

tasks.title: WAVE01 Config: Migrate setup, stats, seed, and adjacent handlers off whole-tree config access
tasks.prompt: Move the next vertical slice of consumers onto narrow immutable policy views. Focus on `handlers/setup`, `handlers/stats`, `seed`, and any adjacent touched paths that currently reach through `configService.Current()` or otherwise consume too much of the config tree. Preserve endpoint behavior while replacing whole-tree reads with narrower configuration slices or consumer-owned interfaces. Keep handlers thin and avoid pushing business policy into HTTP code.
acceptance_criteria: Touched handler and seed paths no longer depend on broad whole-tree config access when a narrower policy view is sufficient.
Setup and stats behavior remain stable for the touched endpoints.
Focused tests cover the migrated narrow-view behavior and protect against regression.
out_of_scope: domain-service migration outside touched consumer paths
notes: Treat `configService.Current()` whole-tree traversal as a smell to remove from touched paths.
Keep setup endpoints semantically stable while narrowing internal dependencies.

tasks.title: WAVE01 Config: Migrate bets, analytics, and markets config dependencies to narrow immutable slices
tasks.prompt: Continue WAVE01 by removing raw `setup`-typed configuration and broad config dependencies from domain-facing consumers, especially bets, analytics, and any directly affected market flows. Replace `*setup.EconomicConfig`, `setup.EconConfigLoader`, and similar leaks with narrow immutable policy slices or consumer-owned interfaces that preserve dependency direction. Keep integer-first economics behavior unchanged and avoid persistence-model leakage.
acceptance_criteria: Touched domain-facing services no longer depend directly on raw `setup` configuration types or loaders.
The migrated consumers use the smallest accounting-safe policy slice or interface they need.
Focused tests prove behavior stability for the touched betting, analytics, or market flows.
out_of_scope: full repository/model decoupling beyond touched paths
notes: Use the smallest accounting-safe config slices possible.
Do not move business policy back into handlers or runtime bootstrap.

tasks.title: WAVE01 Config: Lock economics snapshot semantics and protect accounting correctness
tasks.prompt: Finish the configuration-management wave by making the accounting-sensitive semantics explicit in code and tests. Ensure the touched backend paths treat economics policy as startup-validated and immutable for the running process, reject live-mutation or hot-reload behavior for accounting-sensitive policy, and persist accounting-relevant economics into durable workflow or domain state where correctness requires historical stability. Keep this task tightly scoped to what the touched code can safely support now; if a full persistence decision cannot be completed, implement the highest-value protections and document the exact follow-up boundary.
acceptance_criteria: The touched code and tests make startup-validated immutable economics snapshots explicit for the running process.
No touched path introduces or relies on hot reload for economics or other accounting-sensitive policy.
Accounting-sensitive workflow boundaries touched in this task either persist the required economics values durably or document a precise remaining gap with tests guarding the current behavior.

focus: durability or explicit gap documentation for touched accounting-sensitive flows

notes: This is the WAVE01 stop-and-review point before creating later production-note waves.
Financial and accounting correctness is binary; prefer explicit protection over speculative flexibility.

tasks.title: WAVE02 Logging: Standardize on backend/logger and delete backend/logging
tasks.prompt: Use `lib/design/design-plan.json` and `../socialpredict/README/PRODUCTION-NOTES/BACKEND/02-logging-observability.md` as the binding design context for WAVE02 logging and observability. Complete the first useful slice by migrating the remaining touched backend code off `socialpredict/logging` and onto `socialpredict/logger`, porting or replacing any minimal test seam that truly needs to survive, and deleting `backend/logging`. Preserve current behavior for the touched flows, avoid leaving a compatibility shim behind, and do not replace the logger boundary with direct OpenTelemetry SDK-coupled application code.
acceptance_criteria: No touched non-test backend code imports `socialpredict/logging`.
Any necessary test support is moved or replaced without preserving duplicate package ownership.
`backend/logging` is deleted and focused checks cover the migrated behavior for the touched paths.
No touched path replaces `backend/logger` with a direct application-level OpenTelemetry logging abstraction.

focus: deletion of backend/logging without a compatibility shim
preservation of backend/logger as the application-facing adapter

notes: Treat `backend/logging` as deletion-only scope.
Do not introduce a new wrapper package or long-lived compatibility layer.
Do not solve this task by wiring business code directly to an OpenTelemetry SDK.

tasks.title: WAVE02 Logging: Define the minimal stable backend/logger contract with redaction and OpenTelemetry-aligned guardrails
tasks.prompt: After package unification, harden `backend/logger` as the sole backend runtime log-emission adapter. Make the surviving logger boundary explicit for startup, server, and middleware use; define the minimal stable helper surface, message or field vocabulary expectations, OpenTelemetry-aligned correlation expectations, and redaction or no-secret rules in code and tests; and remove ad hoc patterns that would preserve unsafe or reflection-heavy logging behavior. Keep the work small and runtime-focused; do not start a full structured-logging platform or couple callers directly to exporter-specific APIs.
acceptance_criteria: `backend/logger` exposes a clearer minimal runtime boundary for the touched callers without introducing a second logging abstraction or direct SDK-coupled replacement.
The touched logger contract makes the initial OpenTelemetry-aligned correlation or field vocabulary explicit for the surviving callers.
Touched logging behavior has explicit tests guarding stable runtime usage and safe handling expectations for secrets or sensitive values.
Touched runtime call sites can use the surviving logger package without reintroducing per-package logging dialects.

focus: avoidance of a second logging abstraction

out_of_scope: application metrics changes outside touched callers
notes: Keep logging at runtime and transport boundaries, not in core domain policy.
Never log secrets, passwords, tokens, or API keys.
If JSON logs are emitted outside OTLP, prefer top-level `trace_id`, `span_id`, and `trace_flags`.

tasks.title: WAVE02 Logging: Add lifecycle and request-boundary logging through server and middleware seams
tasks.prompt: With one logger package and a stable contract, add runtime-boundary logging for startup, shutdown or fatal paths, and request lifecycle or correlation where it belongs: `main.go`, `server`, and middleware or security seams. Keep handlers and domain services thin, prefer one vocabulary, use OpenTelemetry-compatible correlation shape at the boundary, and avoid scattering logger construction through business code. Preserve existing endpoint behavior while making deploy, restart, and request diagnostics more deterministic.
acceptance_criteria: Touched startup, server, and middleware paths emit runtime diagnostics through `backend/logger` rather than raw `log` calls or ad hoc local patterns.
Request or correlation context is added at the boundary layer instead of being pushed into domain logic or unrelated handlers.
Any touched serialized correlation fields are compatible with later OpenTelemetry trace-log correlation instead of inventing a second naming scheme.
Focused tests cover the new boundary logging behavior or helpers deterministically for the touched paths.

focus: request or correlation context handling compatible with later tracing
deterministic verification for touched logging helpers

notes: Prefer middleware and runtime-boundary injection over handler-local logger construction.
Keep stdout or stderr-first emission suitable for process and container aggregation.
Do not start a full tracing rollout in this task.

tasks.title: WAVE02 Logging: Harden health and readiness semantics without conflating metrics
tasks.prompt: After runtime logging boundaries are stable, harden the backend's operator-facing health signals. Clarify `/health` and any new readiness or liveness behavior so orchestration and deploy diagnostics can rely on explicit semantics, while keeping `/v0/system/metrics` separate as an application route. Reuse existing runtime and DB seams where practical, keep the implementation deterministic, and avoid inventing a broad observability subsystem or smuggling generic telemetry-backend status into health probes.
acceptance_criteria: Touched server or runtime code exposes explicit, deterministic health or readiness semantics appropriate for orchestration.
Health and readiness handling remains separate from application metrics routes and does not repurpose `/v0/system/metrics` as an infrastructure probe.
Focused route and runtime tests cover the touched health or readiness behavior.

focus: clear runtime-boundary ownership for health and readiness

out_of_scope: Prometheus or external metrics backend rollout
notes: Keep `/health` simple and orchestration-friendly.
Do not collapse health and metrics into one generic observability abstraction.
Do not treat health endpoints as a proxy for telemetry pipeline status.

tasks.title: WAVE02 Logging: Clarify metrics ownership and leave OpenTelemetry tracing or export as a precise follow-up seam
tasks.prompt: Finish the logging and observability wave by tightening the boundary around existing metrics handling after logger unification and health or readiness hardening. Keep `/v0/system/metrics` and adjacent metrics code explicitly application-owned, avoid folding it into runtime probes, and add only the smallest implementation seam or documentation-in-code needed to keep future OpenTelemetry tracing or export work well-scoped. Do not introduce a top-level `observability/` tree, treat a stdout exporter format as a contract, or roll out a speculative tracing framework in this task.
acceptance_criteria: Touched metrics routes or handlers have clearer ownership and do not blur runtime health or readiness with application metrics.
No touched path introduces a new top-level observability package family or a speculative tracing implementation.
The touched code and tests leave a precise, limited seam for later OpenTelemetry tracing or richer metrics work instead of preserving ad hoc coupling.

focus: absence of an observability mega-tree or speculative tracing rollout
precise OpenTelemetry-compatible follow-up seam for later work

out_of_scope: external log or metrics aggregation platform work
notes: This is the WAVE02 stop-and-review point before any richer tracing rollout.
Treat tracing as later than package unification, logger hardening, and health or readiness clarification.
Do not treat an OpenTelemetry stdout exporter format as the production logging contract.
